text:
Three approaches, in decreasing confidence:

**(A) Dualize.** Your two-cell diagnostic says the obstruction lives in source-local restriction breaking $\ker f_\#$-equivariance. Target 2-cochains avoid this tautologically: the Hahn–Banach dual
$$\EFill_f(y) = \sup\{\langle \gamma, P_y\rangle : \gamma\in\Omega^2(S),\ \|f^*d\gamma\|_\infty \le 1\}$$
makes every test object automatically $\ker f_\#$-equivariant. The plan: prove a *dual* EAT — for every feasible $\gamma$, decompose on a randomly translated target cubulation of $S$ at scales $(\delta_1,\delta_2,\delta_3,\delta_4)$ with $\delta_3\delta_4\sim R_3R_4$. Source-side cochains are obtained via $f^*$, never restricted to source bad sets. The $R_1 E_y$ and $E_y^2/(S_1q)$ terms come from comass costs of $f^*d(\text{1-cochain homotopy})$, integrated by Cauchy–Schwarz against $dG$ on $G$-direction cell strands of length $\sim S_1$. The residual is a target-bad term whose pairing with $P_y$ involves $f$-image areas of source plaques. This is conceptually clean and addresses your obstruction by construction; whether the constants close is the actual work.

**(B) Tie $C_y^0$ to $Z_y$ canonically via sheet selection.** The deeper face of your obstruction: arbitrary near-minimizers of $\EFill_f$ have $\partial C_y^0 - Z_y$ an *uncontrolled* $f$-null chain, so there is no geometric link from $C_y^0$ back to $Z_y$, and source-local operations on one don't transfer. Fix by constructing $C_y^0$ so $\partial C_y^0 = W_y$ is a degree-1 sheet of $Z_y$ plus a small switching cycle. The Almgren–De Lellis–Spadaro $Q$-valued framework applied to $G|_{Z_y}: Z_y\to [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]$ gives such a $W_y$ with switching mass $\lesssim \sqrt{E_y\cdot M_{\ge 2}}$, where $M_{\ge 2}$ is the high-$G$-multiplicity mass. Once $W_y\subset Z_y$ is in hand with controlled mass and $f_\#\partial W_y = P_y$, ordinary cubical Federer–Fleming on a $W_y$-filling avoids the quotient issue: the bad set acts on actual geometric source mass. The technical bet is whether $M_{\ge 2}$ admits the right control on $\Omega$; the cutoff $E_y\le 4I/q$ is what makes this plausible.

**(C) Cheap probe: $f$-saturated bad set.** Define $\mathcal{B}^*_z := f^{-1}(f(\mathcal{B}_z))$. Restriction to $\mathcal{B}^*_z$ commutes with $\ker f_\#$ tautologically — it is the pullback of a target set. The Fubini bound now needs $|f^{-1}(f(\text{plaque}))|$ source-controlled, which is plaque-injectivity information. Probably too weak in general, but cheap to verify whether the hard-regime hypotheses force enough local injectivity of $f$ on coordinate 2-plaques.

**Literature.** Almgren's *Big Regularity Paper* §3 and De Lellis–Spadaro *Q-valued functions I/II* for (B); Wenger's quantitative deformation lemmas; Federer §4.1.31 for cellular deformations respecting boundary subgroups. Guth's directional 2-cochain estimates in arXiv:0802.3549 are the closest published precedent for (A). For the dual cochain side, Bourgain–Brezis circulation/divergence Sobolev tools may be needed. Also worth checking: Almgren's varifold tightening, where $f$-equivariance is built in by construction rather than fought.

**Bet.** Try (A) first — it is the only approach that disposes of your obstruction *structurally* rather than circumventing it. (B) is the heavy machinery that should work if the energy accounting cooperates with $Q$-valued theory. The combination — sheet selection feeding a dual cochain inequality — may be what is actually needed if neither alone closes the constants.
